title: Macroeconomics for Investors
summary: GDP, inflation, interest rates, currency, and how macro trends influence markets.
guide:
# Macroeconomics for Developers

## Why Should a Developer Care About Macroeconomics?

Macro affects your career, salary, company's budget, and the tech industry's hiring cycles.
Interest rate rises → VC funding dries up → tech layoffs → fewer jobs.
Inflation → your real salary falls even if nominal salary stays the same.
Recession → company freezes hiring, cuts cloud budgets, kills new projects.

```
2021 (low rates):    2022-2023 (rate hikes):
  Cheap money           Expensive money
  → VC invests freely   → VC pulls back
  → Companies hire      → Companies lay off
  → Salaries spike      → Salaries flatten
  → 'Great Resignation' → 'Survival Mode'

The macro cycle is the tide. You swim better if you understand the tide.
```

---

## Key Macroeconomic Concepts

```
GDP (Gross Domestic Product):
  Total value of goods/services produced in a country in a year.
  Growing GDP = economy expanding = more hiring, more investment
  Shrinking GDP for 2+ quarters = RECESSION = companies cut costs

INFLATION:
  General rise in prices over time.
  Measured by CPI (Consumer Price Index) and PCE.
  Target: ~2% per year (mild inflation = healthy economy)
  > 5%: serious inflation — your savings lose purchasing power

  For developers: salary negotiation timing matters.
  If inflation is 8% and you got 3% raise: real pay cut of 5%.

INTEREST RATES (set by central bank, e.g. Fed in US):
  The cost of borrowing money.
  High rates → borrowing expensive → companies invest less → fewer jobs
  Low rates  → borrowing cheap → companies invest more → more jobs, startups

  Tech industry is uniquely sensitive to interest rates:
  Most startups are funded by borrowed VC money.
  High rates = VCs can earn 5% safe in bonds instead of risking in startups.
  → VC funding to startups drops sharply when rates rise.
```

---

## The Interest Rate Cycle and Tech

```
FEDERAL FUNDS RATE history (simplified):

  2008 (financial crisis): Fed cuts to 0%
  2015-2018: Fed gradually raises
  2020 (COVID): Fed cuts back to 0%
  2022-2023: Fed raises aggressively (0% → 5.5% in 18 months)
  2024: Fed starts cutting

Impact on tech industry:

  0% era (2020-2022):
  └─ Startup valuations 10-100x revenue (growth priced in)
  └─ Hiring frenzy (Netflix, Meta, Google all hiring 10K+/year)
  └─ Remote work salaries jump
  └─ 'Web3', 'metaverse' bubbles inflate

  5.5% era (2022-2024):
  └─ 'Discount Rate' math: future cash flows worth less → valuations crater
  └─ 200K+ tech layoffs in 2022-2023
  └─ IPO market freezes
  └─ VC funding down 60%+
  └─ Startups forced to profitability (good discipline, actually)

Career lesson: low-rate environment = negotiate hard, leverage scarcity.
High-rate environment = focus on stability, demonstrate value, avoid risky startups.
```

---

## Inflation and Your Real Salary

```
REAL vs NOMINAL salary:
  Nominal: the number on your payslip ($100K)
  Real:    purchasing power adjusted for inflation

  2020 salary: $100K
  2021 inflation: 7%
  2022 salary (no raise): $100K nominal
  2022 real salary: $100K / 1.07 = $93,458 in 2020 dollars
  → Real pay cut of 6.5% with no change in nominal salary

TECH SALARIES vs INFLATION:
  2020-2022: tech salaries rose 20-40% (demand > supply of engineers)
  2022-2024: salaries stagnant/declining (layoffs, lower demand)
  Engineers who changed jobs in 2020-2021 saw 30-50% raises.
  Those who stayed at same company: inflation eroded real salary.

LESSON: Understand your real salary = nominal salary / (1+inflation rate)
Negotiate raises ahead of inflation rate to stay neutral.
Change companies every 2-3 years for compensation reset.
```

---

## Cloud and Tech Infrastructure in Economic Context

```
CLOUD SPENDING follows economic cycles:

  Growth phase: companies throw money at cloud for speed
  Recession/rate hike: CFO demands FinOps — cloud bills cut 30-50%

  2022: 'FinOps' became a top priority at every company.
  Cloud waste discovered: unused EC2 instances, over-provisioned RDS,
  dev environments running 24/7, no reserved instances.

  For developers in a downturn: understand cloud costs.
  Knowing AWS cost optimisation = job security in tight markets.
  'This engineer owns cost' = highly valued.

TECH INVESTMENT PRIORITIES shift:
  Bull market: invest in growth (new features, new products)
  Bear market: invest in efficiency (automation, cost cuts, reliable systems)

  Skills more valued in downturns:
    Platform engineering (makes 10 engineers as productive as 15)
    Observability (find and fix expensive inefficiencies)
    AI automation (reduce headcount needed for tasks)
```

---

## Mind Map

```
MACROECONOMICS FOR DEVELOPERS
|
+-- KEY INDICATORS
|   +-- GDP: economy size and growth
|   +-- Inflation: purchasing power erosion
|   +-- Interest rates: cost of money
|   +-- Unemployment: labour market tightness
|
+-- TECH CYCLE
|   +-- Low rates → VC boom → hiring frenzy
|   +-- High rates → VC pullback → layoffs
|   +-- Cycle: ~5-7 years bull → bear → bull
|
+-- YOUR SALARY
|   +-- Real = nominal / (1 + inflation)
|   +-- Job switch = compensation reset
|   +-- Negotiate before rate hikes dry up budgets
|
+-- CLOUD COSTS
    +-- Follows economic cycle (growth vs efficiency)
    +-- FinOps valuable in downturns
    +-- Cost ownership = career security
```

---

## References

### Videos
- **How the Economic Machine Works** by Ray Dalio:
  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PHe0bXAIuk0 (30 min, must watch)
- **Why Tech Layoffs Happened** by PatrickBoyle: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QLQR0UEOUUE

### Articles
- **Principles for Navigating Big Debt Crises** — Ray Dalio (free PDF): https://economicprinciples.org/
- **The Tech Salary Guide** by levels.fyi: https://levels.fyi/

questions.prompt: Why did tech layoffs spike in 2022-2023?
choices: Federal Reserve raised interest rates from 0% to 5.5%. High rates: 1) VCs can earn safe returns in bonds, reducing startup funding. 2) Future revenue worth less (higher discount rate) → valuations crash. 3) Overhired companies cut costs to survive higher borrowing costs.
questions.explanation: Interest rate math: at 0%, a company earning $1M in 5 years has present value $1M (no discount). At 5.5%, discounted back 5 years: $1M / (1.055^5) = $765K. Tech companies priced on future growth get hit hardest. VC funding dried up → startups couldn't raise → layoffs to extend runway.

questions.prompt: Real salary vs nominal salary — you earn $120K in 2023, inflation was 4%. What is your real 2022-equivalent salary?
choices: $115,385 ($120K / 1.04). Your $120K buys the same as $115,385 did in 2022. If you didn't get at least a 4% raise, your purchasing power declined.
questions.explanation: Real salary = nominal / (1 + inflation rate). 4% inflation: $120K / 1.04 = $115,385 in prior-year dollars. Implication: to maintain purchasing power, raises must exceed inflation rate. In 2022 (7% inflation), even a 5% raise was a real pay cut.

questions.prompt: What does the Fed 'lowering interest rates' signal to the tech industry?
choices: Cheaper borrowing: companies invest more, VCs take on more risk (bonds less attractive), startup funding increases, hiring picks up, cloud spending grows. Historically preludes a tech hiring boom.
questions.explanation: Fed rate cuts → lower 'risk-free rate' (bond yields fall). VCs: risk premium over bonds stays same, but absolute returns needed from bonds are lower → startups more attractive. Capital flows to growth assets. 2020: Fed cut to 0% → 2021-2022 hiring frenzy. 2024 cuts → same dynamics beginning.

questions.prompt: What is a recession and how should a developer prepare for one?
choices: Two or more consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth. Developer preparation: build emergency fund (6 months expenses), avoid switching to risky startups near cycle peaks, focus on in-demand skills (cloud costs, observability), demonstrate measurable business impact.
questions.explanation: Recession: GDP shrinks for 2+ quarters. Tech often enters recession sentiment before official declaration if VC funding collapses. Preparation: 1) Emergency fund — layoffs happen fast. 2) In-demand skills (cost efficiency > growth features in downturns). 3) Avoid joining overfunded unprofitable startups late in bull cycles.

questions.prompt: 'FinOps' became critical in 2022-2023 because:
choices: Rate hikes forced companies to cut cloud bills 30-50%. Cloud waste discovered: idle instances, over-provisioned databases, no reserved instances. Engineers who could own and reduce cloud costs became highly valued during budget cuts.
questions.explanation: FinOps = financial operations for cloud. In growth mode: cloud bill is an afterthought. In survival mode: CFO demands cloud bill cut by 30%. Engineering teams audited idle resources, right-sized instances, bought Reserved Instances, killed dev environments overnight. Cloud cost ownership = job security in downturns.

questions.prompt: What is the 'discount rate' and why does it make tech valuations sensitive to interest rates?
choices: The rate used to calculate present value of future cash flows. High discount rate: future revenue worth much less today. Tech companies valued on future growth get destroyed when rates rise — their future profits are discounted heavily.
questions.explanation: PV = FV / (1+r)^n. $1M in year 5: at r=0%, PV=$1M. At r=10%, PV=$1M/1.1^5=$621K. Tech companies priced at 20x future revenue: at 0% rates = $20M valuation. Same company at 10% rates: future revenue discounted → $12M valuation. Same business, 40% value drop purely from rate change.

questions.prompt: What cycle length should developers use for career planning?
choices: Economic cycles average 5-10 years (boom to bust to boom). Tech hiring cycles: 2015-2019 boom, 2020-2022 boom, 2022-2023 bust. Plan: switch companies every 2-3 years for salary resets, build savings in boom times for recessions.
questions.explanation: Tech hiring cycles: tend to follow Fed policy and VC sentiment cycles. Boom (2020-2021): negotiate aggressively, multiple offers viable. Bust (2022-2023): stability > optimisation. Recovery (2024+): pick up pace of negotiation again. Engineers who stay flat during boom and switch during recovery miss compensation gains.

questions.prompt: Inflation at 6% and you received a 4% raise. What happened to your real compensation?
choices: Real compensation fell approximately 2% (4% nominal - 6% inflation). In 2022: many tech workers received no raise while inflation hit 8-9% — effective 8-9% pay cut.
questions.explanation: Approximate real raise = nominal raise % - inflation %. 4% - 6% = -2% real. Exact: (1.04/1.06) - 1 = -1.89%. Over 3 years of this: significant purchasing power erosion. Active job seekers in 2022 got 20-40% raises beating inflation. Those who stayed: 10-15% real pay cut over 2 years.

questions.prompt: Why is understanding cloud spending important during economic downturns?
choices: Companies aggressively cut cloud costs in downturns. Engineers who own cloud efficiency: job-protecting skill. Cloud bills: often 20-40% of a startup's burn rate. Cutting 30% = extending runway 3-6 months. That's survival.
questions.explanation: Startup burn rate example: $500K/month total, $150K is AWS. Cutting AWS by 30% = $45K/month saved = $540K/year. At current burn: 1+ extra month of runway. For a startup trying to reach profitability: cloud optimisation can be the difference between survival and shutdown.

questions.prompt: Compound learning analogy to compound interest — what is the key insight?
choices: Knowledge compounds: knowing A makes learning B faster (B builds on A), which makes C faster. 1% daily improvement = 37x in a year. Early learning investments (fundamentals) have highest compounding leverage. Skipping fundamentals = slower compounding.
questions.explanation: Investment: $1 growing 1%/day for 365 days = $37.78. Knowledge: understand TCP/IP → learning HTTP much easier. Know HTTP → learning REST APIs faster. Know REST → learning GraphQL faster. Foundational knowledge has the most compounding power. This is why CS fundamentals matter even for web developers.

questions.prompt: Should you join a funded startup during a bear market (high interest rates)?
choices: Always yes — startups are always better

High risk: VC funding is tight, runway may be limited, layoffs likely if they fail to raise next round. Unless you have equity that could be valuable at credible exit, the risk/reward may favour stable employment. Well-funded profitable startups: different.
questions.explanation: During high-rate environment: startup funding down 60%+, valuations compressed = equity worth less. Startups that raised at 2021 valuations: equity underwater. Solid company with 18+ months runway and path to profitability: acceptable. Pre-revenue startup burning $500K/month with 9 months runway: significant risk.

questions.prompt: What macroeconomic indicator most directly predicts tech hiring?
choices: Venture capital funding flows. VC investment leads hiring by 6-12 months: when VCs invest heavily, companies that raise then hire aggressively 6 months later. VC funding is itself driven by: interest rates, exits/IPOs market, LP returns.
questions.explanation: Tech hiring chain: Fed rates → bond yields → VC fund returns → VC investment → startup hiring. VC data is public (Crunchbase, PitchBook) with a quarter lag. VC funding in Q3 frequently predicts hiring intensity in Q1 of following year. Tracking VC activity = forward indicator of tech job market.

questions.prompt: What is 'nominal' vs 'real' GDP growth?
choices: Nominal GDP: total value in current dollars — includes inflation effect. Real GDP: adjusted for inflation. If nominal GDP grew 5% but inflation was 4%, real GDP growth was only 1%. Recessions defined by real GDP.
questions.explanation: Nominal GDP can rise while economy shrinks if inflation is high enough. Real GDP growth = nominal GDP growth - inflation rate (approximately). 2022 US: nominal GDP appeared positive, but real GDP contracted two quarters in a row — technically a recession by some definitions, though official NBER didn't call it one.

questions.prompt: Developer skill most valued in economic downturns vs upturns:
choices: Upturn: growth features, new products, speed of delivery. Downturn: cost reduction, reliability, observability, automation (reduce headcount), FinOps. Platform engineers (enable others) valued in downturns. Feature developers more valued in growth phases.
questions.explanation: Recession hiring: 'do more with less'. Platform engineering (CI/CD, developer tools) = 1 infra engineer enables 10 product engineers = high leverage. Observability = find and fix expensive issues. AI automation = reduce manual work costs. Feature development slows when product investment pauses.

questions.prompt: What is the 'venture capital funding cycle' and why does it follow interest rates?
choices: VC LPs (pension funds, endowments) have target returns. At 0% rates: bonds return 0%, VC at 15% expected return looks great. At 5% rates: bonds safe at 5%, VC must return 20%+ to compensate risk. Higher hurdle → VCs more selective → less funding → fewer startups hired.
questions.explanation: VC as an asset class competes with other investments. Opportunity cost: LP chooses between bond (safe 5%) and VC fund (risky 15%). When bond yields rise, VC must offer higher returns to justify allocation. Higher return requirements = VCs invest less, more selectively, at lower valuations. 2022: VC investment fell 60%+ globally.

questions.prompt: What is 'burn rate' and why do developers need to understand it?
choices: Rate at which a startup spends its cash reserve, usually measured monthly. Runway = cash / burn rate. High burn rate + low runway = company may shut down or do emergency layoffs. Developer: join companies with 18+ months runway.
questions.explanation: Startup: $3M raised, burns $300K/month = 10 months runway. Needs to raise or hit profitability in 10 months or fold. In 2022-2023 rate environment, many startups couldn't raise next round at acceptable valuation → layoffs or shutdown. Asking 'what's your runway?' before joining a startup is prudent.

questions.prompt: Why do cloud providers like AWS benefit from both bull and bear markets?
choices: They don't benefit in bear markets

Bull: companies use cloud for rapid scaling without capital outlay. Bear: FinOps — companies audit and shift from owned hardware to optimised cloud (consolidation, reserved instances). Cloud is essential infrastructure in both phases.
They only benefit in bull markets
questions.explanation: AWS 2022-2023: slowed growth but didn't collapse. Companies couldn't cut cloud entirely (apps run there). They optimised: reserved instances (commit to usage for discount), spot instances, rightsizing. Net: cloud spending more efficient, not eliminated. AWS earns less per workload but workloads stay.

questions.prompt: What does 'quantitative tightening' (QT) mean and how does it affect developers?
choices: Fed selling bonds from its balance sheet, reducing money supply. Makes credit tighter, borrowing more expensive (reinforces rate hikes). Effect: even slower startup funding, lower evaluations, tech spending cuts.
questions.explanation: 2020-2021: Fed did QE (buying bonds, expanding balance sheet, pumping money). 2022+: QT (selling bonds, shrinking money supply, removing liquidity). Double impact on tech: 1) higher rates → higher borrowing costs. 2) QT → less money sloshing around looking for returns → less VC allocation to tech.

questions.prompt: What should you do with savings during a tech bull market (low rates, hiring frenzy)?
choices: Build emergency fund (6+ months expenses), invest consistently (never try to time markets), avoid lifestyle inflation, build negotiating power through skills. Good times are when to prepare for bad times.
Quit and start a company immediately
questions.explanation: Bull market behaviour: salary high, bonuses large, stock vesting well. Risk: lifestyle inflation (expensive rent, car, etc.) that's hard to reverse. Preparation: automate savings (pay yourself first), build 6-12 month emergency fund, index fund investments, pay down high-interest debt. When downturn hits: you have runway.

questions.prompt: How does the stock market relate to tech hiring, and is it a reliable indicator?
choices: Stock market is a leading indicator (forward-looking) but imperfect. NASDAQ down 30%+ = tech company valuations down → hiring freeze typically follows 3-6 months later. But: profitable big tech (Google, Microsoft) can absorb stock drops better than unprofitable startups.
questions.explanation: Correlation not causation. NASDAQ crashed in 2022: tech hiring froze with 3-6 month lag. But: large profitable tech (Microsoft, Apple) hired more slowly, not emergency cuts. Unprofitable growth startups: immediate cuts. Better indicator than stock market: VC funding rounds, startup job postings, and weekly earnings calls from major tech companies.

flashcards.back: Real salary = nominal salary / (1 + inflation rate). Approximate: real raise = nominal raise % - inflation %. If inflation=7% and raise=3%: real pay cut of ~4%. To stay neutral: raise must equal inflation rate. Job switch is the fastest way to reset.

flashcards.front: Fed rate impact on tech hiring
flashcards.back: Rates ↑ → bond yields ↑ → VC opportunity cost ↑ → less VC funding → startups lay off or freeze hiring. Rates ↓ → bonds less attractive → VC invests more → hiring picks up. Lag: 6-12 months from rate change to hiring impact.

Bull (low rates): negotiate aggressively, switch companies for 20-30% raises, stock options have positive momentum. Bear (high rates): prioritise stability, build cash reserves, focus on efficiency skills (cloud cost, platform eng), avoid late-stage underfunded startups.

Runway months = total cash / monthly burn rate. < 12 months: risky time to join. 18+ months: reasonable stability. 36+ months: very stable. Before joining: ask 'what's the runway?' and 'what are the next milestones?' Profitable = no runway concern.

Bear market: CFO mandates cloud bill cuts 30-50%. Valuable skills: identify idle resources, rightsize instances, buy Reserved Instances, eliminate dev/test waste, implement auto-shutdown. AWS cost reduction = extending company runway. Knowing this = job security.

1% better daily = 37x in a year (1.01^365). Knowledge builds on knowledge: TCP→HTTP→REST→GraphQL each easier because of prior. Invest in fundamentals early (highest compounding leverage). Teach others: solidifies your own knowledge AND builds reputation.

Two or more consecutive quarters of REAL GDP growth being negative. Real GDP = nominal GDP adjusted for inflation. Recession signals for developers: VC funding falls, companies freeze headcount, cloud budgets cut. Typically: hiring lags GDP by 2-3 quarters.

PV = FV / (1+r)^years. At r=0%: $1M in 5 years = $1M today. At r=10%: $1M in 5 years = $621K today. Tech companies priced on future growth: high rates → future earnings worth much less → valuations crash. Direct cause of 2022 tech valuation collapse.

project.brief: It is Q1 2026. The Fed has cut rates twice from their 2023 peak. You need to make the following decisions: 1) Should you negotiate a salary increase now or wait 6 months? 2) A Series B startup (18 months runway) is offering you 20% more salary + 0.5% equity. Should you take it? 3) Your company is asking you to help cut cloud bills by 30%. Where would you start? Reason through each using macroeconomic principles.

checklist.text: Salary timing: Fed cuts signal hiring recovery in 6-12 months. Negotiate now while the next cycle begins, before competition increases.
Startup analysis: 18 months runway + Series B = reasonable stability. Check: burn rate vs MRR growth, path to profitability. Equity: 0.5% of what valuation? Model exit scenarios.
Cloud cuts: start with idle/overnight dev environments (often 20-30% waste). Then: rightsize over-provisioned RDS/ElastiCache. Buy Reserved Instances for steady-state workloads.
Real salary: current inflation ~3%. Negotiate at minimum 3% + performance component to maintain purchasing power.
Equity discounting: startup equity has discount rate too. Apply personal discount rate (opportunity cost) to determine if 0.5% compensates for job security risk differential.

hints: Equity value: 0.5% × (potential exit valuation) × (probability of exit) × (probability of staying to vest) × (dilution discount). Very rough: at $20M exit, 0.5% = $100K pre-tax before dilution.
FinOps quick wins: AWS Trusted Advisor identifies low-hanging fruit. Cost Explorer by service. Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering. Delete unattached EBS volumes.
Negotiation timing: early in a rate-cutting cycle (now) = leverage improving. Competition for engineers has been low, about to rise. Negotiate before the market gets competitive again.
